ai_strategy.summary: Columbia AI is a university-wide initiative powered by the Data Science Institute and driven by Columbia Engineering, the Office of the Executive Vice President for Research, and DSI. Columbia also has a detailed Provost generative AI policy covering teaching, learning, research, procurement, disclosure, bias, confidentiality, and data security.

key_provisions: Use AI in accordance with academic integrity expectations and instructor or unit guidance
Do not enter restricted, confidential, regulated, or sensitive data into unapproved AI tools
Review AI outputs for accuracy, hallucination, bias, copyright, and attribution issues
Disclose or cite AI use when required by course, research, publisher, or unit rules
Keep human judgment and accountability in consequential academic, research, and administrative decisions
ai_policy.summary: Columbia University's AI policy posture is best characterized as comprehensive: public sources show explicit expectations for responsible use, privacy, disclosure, academic integrity, and human review, while leaving some implementation decisions to units or instructors.

initiatives.title: Provost generative AI policy
initiatives.description: Columbia's policy applies to staff, faculty, students, and researchers, requires disclosure and accuracy review, restricts confidential and personal data, addresses bias, and routes AI procurement through IT review.

initiatives.title: Google Gemini and NotebookLM secure campus rollout
initiatives.description: CUIT pages identify Google Gemini and NotebookLM as generative AI tools available through Columbia accounts, adding supported AI-search, writing, and research workflows to the institutionally governed tool portfolio.
initiatives.significance: This closes a platform evidence gap by moving Columbia beyond policy-only references to named supported AI platforms.

investment.description: No public seat count or contract value disclosed.

collaborations.description: Google tools are available through Columbia account infrastructure.

tools_and_vendors.name: NotebookLM

initiatives.title: Empire AI student-preparation assessment grant
initiatives.description: Columbia is part of the Empire AI consortium grant from Google.org to expand multi-institution assessment of preparing students for an AI-driven workforce.
initiatives.significance: This is a public, outcomes-oriented student-preparation signal tied to a disclosed external grant.

investment.description: Grant totals more than $1 million for the consortium.

regulatory_context.summary: Columbia University operates in New York; public AI guidance should be read alongside applicable state, federal, and institutional privacy, security, accessibility, and academic-integrity requirements.

narrative_summary: Columbia University (New York, NY) is an R1 institution whose public AI posture shows activity across governance, teaching, research, enterprise tooling, workforce readiness, and access. Columbia AI is a university-wide initiative powered by the Data Science Institute and driven by Columbia Engineering, the Office of the Executive Vice President for Research, and DSI. Columbia also has a detailed Provost generative AI policy covering teaching, learning, research, procurement, disclosure, bias, confidentiality, and data security. The most concrete public initiatives captured here are Columbia AI university-wide initiative; Provost generative AI policy; AI for Sciences and Engineering Center; Columbia Engineering AI executive certificate; AI syllabus and teaching resources. Across the profile, the strongest evidence comes from official university AI hubs, policy pages, teaching resources, research-center pages, and program pages rather than secondary reporting. The institution_type is set to r1_research from the canonical Carnegie R1 list. Known gaps are intentionally explicit: leadership names, user counts, detailed budgets, and compute counts are only included when they surfaced in public sources. Overall, the profile supports at least five AI initiatives and evidences strategy/leadership, governance, teaching/curriculum, research infrastructure, student services or enterprise tooling, workforce/community, partnerships, and equity dimensions where public sources permit.
